title: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases
authors: Stephen L. Hauser, Arnold R. Kriegstein, Stanley B. Prusiner

items.subtopic: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases | NEUROIMMUNOLOGY AND NEUROINFLAMMATION
items.title: Role of Glial Cells in Neuroinflammation and Neurodegeneration
items.content: The intrinsic cellular components within the CNS, such as microglia/macrophages, astrocytes, and oligodendrocytes, play crucial roles in mediating tissue damage. These cells are central to understanding neuroinflammatory processes and their impact on a wide range of brain disorders.
keyPoints: Microglia/macrophages and astrocytes are key mediators of tissue damage in the CNS.
Oligodendrocytes are recognized as central players across various brain disorders.
Non-cell-autonomous pathologies, resulting from extensive communication networks between these cell types, likely underlie many human CNS disorders.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases | NEUROIMMUNOLOGY AND NEUROINFLAMMATION

items.subtopic: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases | NEUROIMMUNOLOGY AND NEUROINFLAMMATION
items.title: Expanding Scope of Neuroimmunology in Neurodegenerative Disorders
items.content: Neuroimmunology has evolved to include the study of neuroinflammation and innate immune system roles in a broader category of neurological disorders, particularly neurodegenerative diseases. This shift highlights the importance of understanding cellular interactions within the CNS.
keyPoints: Recognition that neuroinflammation is crucial in many neurologic disorders, especially neurodegenerative ones.
Innate immune system components are integral to these processes.
Focus on cell types like microglia/macrophages and astrocytes for their roles in CNS damage.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases | NEUROIMMUNOLOGY AND NEUROINFLAMMATION

items.title: Role and Replacement of Microglia in the CNS
items.content: Microglia, derived from primitive macrophages, are crucial for antigen presentation and innate immunity in the nervous system. They primarily maintain their numbers through self-renewal but can be replaced by peripherally derived macrophages under certain conditions.
keyPoints: Derived from yolk sac macrophages early in embryogenesis
Maintain numbers via self-renewal, not repopulation from circulation
Can be replaced by peripheral macrophages under specific conditions
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROGLIA AND MACROPHAGES (part 1)

items.title: Microglial Activation and Heterogeneity
items.content: Microglia exist in 'resting' and 'activated' states, with activation primed by factors like type 1 interferons. Single-cell transcriptome sequencing reveals significant heterogeneity among microglia.
keyPoints: Resting state: extensively ramified appearance
Activated state: globular amoeboid phenotype
Priming by type 1 interferons leads to hyperresponsiveness
Single-cell sequencing shows high heterogeneity
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROGLIA AND MACROPHAGES (part 1)

items.title: Microglia in Neurodegenerative Diseases and Therapeutic Targets
items.content: Microglia play a primary role in neurodegeneration, with complement cascade activation being central to Alzheimer's disease pathogenesis. Genetic evidence supports microglial involvement, particularly through TREM2.
keyPoints: Complement proteins C1q and C3 tag synapses for elimination
Knockout of C1q or C3 can rescue neurodegeneration abnormalities
TREM2 mutations increase AD risk up to fourfold
Clinical trials include inhibitory monoclonal antibody against C1q
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROGLIA AND MACROPHAGES (part 1)

items.title: Role of Progranulin in Frontotemporal Dementia
items.content: Progranulin, encoded by the GRN gene, is crucial for lysosomal function and microglial homeostasis. Mutations leading to haploinsufficiency are highly penetrant for FTD, causing age-dependent microglial activation and upregulation of proinflammatory cytokines.
keyPoints: ~10% of all cases of frontotemporal degeneration (FTD) are due to heterozygous mutations in the GRN gene.
Progranulin is essential for lysosomal function and microglial homeostasis.
Inhibition of complement activation can rescue deficits caused by progranulin deficiency.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROGLIA AND MACROPHAGES (part 2)

items.title: Microglia's Role in Neuroinflammation and Antigen Presentation
items.content: Microglia, as antigen-presenting cells, express class II MHC molecules for CD4 T cell interaction. In PD, microglial antigen presentation may trigger autoimmune responses against α-synuclein. Microglia also interact with T and B lymphocytes, influencing neuroinflammation.
keyPoints: Microglia are the primary source of antigen-presenting cells in the brain.
Neurotoxic T cells reactive to α-synuclein epitopes are common in Parkinson's disease (PD).
Secretion of CCL3 by microglia promotes recruitment of memory CD8 T cells, seen in conditions like multiple sclerosis (MS).
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROGLIA AND MACROPHAGES (part 2)

items.title: Astrocyte Functions and Neuroinflammation in CNS Disorders
items.content: Astrocytes, comprising over half of all cells in the CNS, have diverse roles beyond structural support. They regulate synapse development and maintenance through secretion of factors like apolipoprotein E and thrombospondins. Astrocytes also modulate vascular tone and maintain blood-brain barrier integrity via aquaporin-4 channels. In neuroimmune disorders such as MS, astrocytes can promote inflammation by secreting chemokines (CCL2, CXCL10) in response to cytokines like GM-CSF from lymphocytes. They may also support pathogenic B cells through BAFF production.
keyPoints: Astrocytes represent half or more of all CNS cells.
Secrete factors regulating synapse development (e.g., apolipoprotein E, thrombospondins).
Modulate vascular tone and blood-brain barrier integrity via aquaporin-4 channels.
In MS, astrocytes secrete chemokines CCL2 and CXCL10 in response to GM-CSF.
Promote survival of pathogenic B cells through BAFF production.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ASTROCYTES

items.title: Astrocyte Reactivity and Disease Mechanisms in Neurodegenerative Disorders
items.content: Reactive astrocytes exhibit transcriptional and functional heterogeneity, influencing inflammation, neurotoxicity, or repair. In conditions like MS, AD, PD, and ALS, reactive astrocytes can exacerbate disease through various mechanisms. For example, in ALS models with SOD1 and C9orf72 mutations, astrocyte expression of these genes promotes neurotoxicity. In AD, astrocytes contribute to amyloid accumulation via apolipoprotein E. Reactive astrocytes may lose synapse phagocytosis ability and become toxic through complement-mediated damage or free radical production.
keyPoints: Reactive astrocytes show transcriptional and functional heterogeneity.
Influence inflammation, neurotoxicity, or repair in CNS disorders.
In ALS models with SOD1/C9orf72 mutations, astrocyte expression promotes neurotoxicity.
In AD, contribute to amyloid accumulation via apolipoprotein E.
May become toxic through complement-mediated damage and free radical production.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ASTROCYTES

items.title: Role of Oligodendrocytes in Myelination and Neuroprotection
items.content: Oligodendrocytes are crucial for myelinating multiple axons in the CNS, enhancing nerve conduction efficiency and providing neuroprotective support. Their differentiation and extent of myelination are influenced by neuronal activity through adaptive myelination.
keyPoints: A single oligodendrocyte can ensheath multiple axons in the CNS.
Ongoing neuronal activity influences both oligodendrocyte differentiation and the extent of myelination (adaptive myelination).
Myelin provides critical neuroprotective support to axons.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · OLIGODENDROCYTES AND MYELIN

items.title: Mechanisms and Consequences of Myelin Pathology
items.content: Disorders in myelin, whether due to genetic mutations or autoimmune responses, contribute significantly to neurologic conditions. These pathologies can lead to cognitive changes, including dementia, and are implicated in diseases like multiple sclerosis (MS) and Alzheimer's disease.
keyPoints: Inherited mutations in myelin proteins cause several clinically important neurological disorders.
Autoimmune demyelinating disorders target myelin due to molecular similarities with microbial cell wall constituents.
Alterations in myelin are linked to cognitive changes associated with aging, including dementia.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · OLIGODENDROCYTES AND MYELIN

items.title: Regulation of Oligodendrocyte Differentiation and Myelination
items.content: Several molecules regulate oligodendrocyte differentiation and myelination, with some inhibiting and others promoting these processes. These pathways are potential targets for therapies aimed at myelin repair.
keyPoints: LINGO-1, hyaluronan, chondroitin sulfate proteoglycan, Wnt pathway, Notch (Jagged), fibrinogen, and M1 muscarinic receptor Chrm1 inhibit oligodendrocyte differentiation.
Retinoic acid receptor RXR γ, vitamin D, and thyroid hormone promote oligodendrocyte maturation.
Chrm1 knockout in EAE model improved remyelination and axonal protection.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · OLIGODENDROCYTES AND MYELIN

items.subtopic: LYMPHATICS OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.title: Role of Glymphatic System in CNS Debris Clearance
items.content: The glymphatic system, involving astroglial cells and aquaporin-4 channels, facilitates the clearance of solutes and debris from the brain interstitium. This process is enhanced during sleep, which aids in meeting the high metabolic demands of the nervous system.
keyPoints: Glymphatic system involves CSF circulation through astrocyte foot processes rich in aquaporin-4 water channels.
Interstitial flow and debris clearance are impaired with aging due to changes in aquaporin-4 expression.
Sleep accelerates glymphatic transport, enhancing clearance of neurodegenerative disease-associated proteins like $ \beta $-amyloid.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · LYMPHATICS OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: LYMPHATICS OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.title: Impact of Sleep on Glymphatic Function and Neurodegeneration
items.content: Sleep plays a critical role in the glymphatic system's function, promoting the clearance of metabolic waste such as $ \beta $-amyloid. Disruption of sleep can lead to increased accumulation of amyloid plaques, exacerbating neurodegenerative conditions like Alzheimer's disease.
keyPoints: Sleep enhances fluid and solute transport in the glymphatic system.
In AD mouse models, sleep deprivation increases $ \beta $-amyloid plaque accumulation.
Glymphatic pathways facilitate lymphocyte egress and antigen encounters, potentially influencing immune responses.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · LYMPHATICS OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: LYMPHATICS OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.title: Distinct Pathways for CNS Lymphatic Drainage
items.content: The CNS has two distinct lymphatic drainage pathways: the glymphatic system, which removes waste from the brain interstitium, and a plexus of lymphatic-like vessels associated with meningeal arteries and deep dural sinuses, important for clearing solutes from CSF.
keyPoints: Glymphatic system clears interstitial waste; sinus-associated lymphoid structures clear CSF solutes.
Deep cervical lymph nodes may be involved in antigen-specific immune responses in MS.
The exact functions and interrelationships of these systems are still being explored.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · LYMPHATICS OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.title: Role of Microbiota in Autoimmune Neurologic Diseases
items.content: The human microbiome, consisting of $10^{14}$ organisms, influences nervous system development and function. Gut microbes shape immune responses by interacting with human metabolism, affecting autoimmune neurologic diseases like multiple sclerosis (MS). Germ-free mice or those treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics show resistance to experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis (EAE), linked to altered cytokine production and increased regulatory T cells.
keyPoints: Human microbiome consists of $10^{14}$ organisms, outnumbering human genes by 100-fold.
Germ-free mice or those treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics are resistant to EAE.
Resistance is associated with decreased proinflammatory cytokines and increased IL-10, IL-13, regulatory T cells.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROBIOTA AND NEUROLOGIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Molecular Mimicry in Neurologic Disorders
items.content: Microbial proteins can trigger cross-reactive immune responses against homologous nervous system proteins through molecular mimicry. This mechanism is implicated in diseases like neuromyelitis optica, multiple sclerosis, Guillain-Barré syndrome, and narcolepsy, where microbial antigens resemble neural components.
keyPoints: Molecular mimicry involves cross-reactive immune responses against homologous nervous system proteins.
Examples include aquaporin-4 in neuromyelitis optica, HLA molecules with A. muciniphila peptides in MS, Gm1 ganglioside in Guillain-Barré syndrome, and hypocretin in narcolepsy.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROBIOTA AND NEUROLOGIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Microbiota on Behavior and CNS Function
items.content: The gut microbiome influences behavior and central nervous system function through various mechanisms. Germ-free mice exhibit altered anxiety, stress response, exploratory behaviors, and memory formation due to changes in gene expression related to neural signaling and neurotransmitter modulation. These effects can be reversed by co-housing with non-germ-free mice. The vagus nerve plays a role in mediating these behavioral changes, as demonstrated by the impact of Lactobacillus rhamnosus on GABA1b expression and anxiety-like behaviors.
keyPoints: Germ-free mice show altered behavior linked to gene expression changes in neural signaling pathways.
Behavioral effects can be reversed by co-housing with non-germ-free mice.
Vagus nerve implicated in mediating behavioral changes, as seen with Lactobacillus rhamnosus affecting GABA1b and anxiety.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROBIOTA AND NEUROLOGIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Gut Microbiome's Role in Ameliorating ASD-like Behaviors
items.content: Manipulation of the gut microbiome, specifically through oral treatment with Bacteroides fragilis, has shown potential to correct autistic behaviors and improve gastrointestinal dysfunction in preclinical models. These findings have led to preliminary clinical trials exploring fecal gut transplantation in children with ASD.
keyPoints: Proinflammatory cytokine IL-17 implicated in producing ASD-like changes.
Oral treatment of offspring with Bacteroides fragilis corrected autistic behaviors and improved gastrointestinal dysfunction in mice models.
Small uncontrolled study on fecal gut transplantation in children with ASD reported encouraging results but requires confirmation in controlled trials.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROBIOTA AND NEUROLOGIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Microbiome Influence on Parkinson's Disease Pathogenesis
items.content: The gut microbiome may play a role in the pathogenesis of Parkinson's disease, potentially through vagal transport of misfolded alpha-synuclein. This hypothesis is supported by models showing that severing the vagus nerve blocks the deposition of alpha-synuclein in the brain.
keyPoints: Injection of misfolded alpha-synuclein into the gut triggered its deposition in the brain, blocked by severing the vagus nerve.
Gut origin hypothesis for PD supported by clinical and pathologic studies; epidemiologic data suggest vagotomy may be protective against PD.
Transplantation of intestinal microbiota from PD patients worsened motor deficits and neuroinflammation in transgenic mice models.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROBIOTA AND NEUROLOGIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Role of Protein Aggregation in Neurodegeneration
items.content: Protein aggregation, including plaques and neurofibrillary tangles, is a hallmark of neurodegenerative diseases. These aggregates sequester toxic proteins, potentially reducing their harmful effects.
keyPoints: Protein aggregates may become less harmful when sequestered into structures like plaques and NFTs.
Mutations in the APP gene increase Aβ42 production, which is more prone to prion-like conformation than Aβ40.
Reduced production of β-amyloid protects against Alzheimer's Disease (AD).
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ☑ PROTEIN AGGREGATION AND CELL DEATH (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Factors in Parkinson’s Disease and Related Disorders
items.content: Mutations in specific genes are linked to familial forms of Parkinson's disease (PD) and related disorders, influencing the onset and progression.
keyPoints: Mutation in α-synuclein gene identified in a Greek family linked to PD.
Genes like LRRK2, parkin, PINK1, and DJ-1 modify PD onset.
Parkin is a ubiquitin ligase causing autosomal recessive early-onset PD-like illness.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ☑ PROTEIN AGGREGATION AND CELL DEATH (part 1)

items.title: Mitochondrial Dysfunction in Neurodegenerative Diseases
items.content: Mitochondrial dynamics, including fission and fusion processes, are crucial for mitochondrial health. Dysfunctions in these processes contribute to neurodegeneration.
keyPoints: Mutations affecting mitochondrial dynamics can lead to diseases like Friedreich's ataxia.
Drp1 is involved in mitochondrial fission; MFN1/2 and OPA1 are responsible for fusion.
β-amyloid and mutant huntingtin protein induce mitochondrial fragmentation, leading to neuronal cell death.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ☑ PROTEIN AGGREGATION AND CELL DEATH (part 1)

items.title: Role of Protein Aggregates in Neurodegenerative Diseases
items.content: Protein aggregates, particularly oligomers, are implicated in neuronal death across neurodegenerative diseases. The exact number of monomers forming disease-specific oligomers is unknown. These aggregates, often ubiquitinated, may lead to cellular dysfunction and apoptosis if not degraded by the proteasome.
keyPoints: Oligomers are small-protein aggregates central to neurodegenerative diseases.
The specific number of monomers in disease-specific oligomers is elusive.
Protein aggregates are ubiquitinated for degradation by the 26S proteasome.
Impaired degradation can lead to cellular dysfunction and apoptosis.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ☑ PROTEIN AGGREGATION AND CELL DEATH (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Autophagy in Neurodegenerative Diseases
items.content: Autophagy, crucial for degrading protein aggregates, is impaired in diseases like AD, PD, FTD, and HD. In HD, cargo recognition failure contributes to aggregate formation and cell death. Mutations in glucocerebrosidase (GBA) are linked to 5% of all PD cases and 8-9% of dementia with Lewy bodies, highlighting lysosomal dysfunction.
keyPoints: Autophagy is vital for degrading protein aggregates in neurodegenerative diseases.
Impaired autophagy contributes to cell death in AD, PD, FTD, and HD.
In HD, cargo recognition failure leads to aggregate formation and cell death.
GBA mutations are associated with 5% of all PD cases and 8-9% of dementia with Lewy bodies.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ☑ PROTEIN AGGREGATION AND CELL DEATH (part 2)

items.title: Prion Etiology of Neurodegenerative Diseases
items.content: Prions, which are infectious proteins devoid of nucleic acids, play a crucial role in the pathogenesis of several neurodegenerative diseases. They cause abnormal folding and aggregation of normal host-encoded proteins into amyloid fibrils.
keyPoints: Prions are composed of misfolded proteins that can propagate by inducing misfolding in normal proteins.
Diseases like kuru, Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD), and scrapie are caused by PrP^Sc prions.
Amyloid plaques in Alzheimer's Disease (AD) contain Aβ prions, linking genetic mutations in the APP gene to familial AD.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · PRIONS AND NEURODEGENERATIVE DISEASES (part 1)

items.title: Late Onset of Familial Neurodegenerative Diseases
items.content: The late onset of familial neurodegenerative diseases may be due to the accumulation of mutant prion proteins at an advanced age, as protein quality-control mechanisms decline with age.
keyPoints: Mutant proteins in heritable neurodegenerative diseases are expressed early but manifest later in life.
The late onset might result from a second event where mutant prions begin to accumulate due to diminished protein quality control in older individuals.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · PRIONS AND NEURODEGENERATIVE DISEASES (part 1)

items.title: Double-Prion Diseases
items.content: Alzheimer's Disease and other conditions are characterized as double-prion diseases, involving both Aβ and tau prions. This dual presence is also observed in Down syndrome patients with AD and certain populations developing ALS with dementia.
keyPoints: AD is identified as a double-prion disease due to the coexistence of Aβ and tau prions.
Both Aβ and tau prions are found in the brains of AD patients, Down syndrome individuals with AD, and Chamorro people on Guam and Japanese on the Kii peninsula with ALS and dementia.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · PRIONS AND NEURODEGENERATIVE DISEASES (part 1)

items.title: α-Synuclein Prions and Disease Progression
items.content: Inoculation of MSA patient brains into Tg(SCNA A53T) mice results in death approximately 90 days post-intracerebral inoculation, whereas spontaneous mutant α-synuclein (A53T) prions in Tg mouse brains lead to recipient Tg mouse mortality around 200 days.
keyPoints: MSA patient brain inoculation into Tg(SCNA A53T) mice results in death ~90 days post-inoculation
Mutant α-synuclein (A53T) prions spontaneously form and kill recipient Tg mice ~200 days after formation
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · PRIONS AND NEURODEGENERATIVE DISEASES (part 2)

items.title: Prion Strain Variability and Conformation
items.content: Strain-specific variations in prions are encoded in the conformation of PrP^Sc, with neuroanatomical deposition patterns dependent on prion strain. Evidence supports this for PrP, Aβ, tau, and α-synuclein prions.
keyPoints: Prion strains produce distinct clinical presentations and neuropathologic lesions
Strain-specific variation is encoded in the conformation of PrP^Sc
Neuroanatomical patterns of prion deposition depend on prion strain
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · PRIONS AND NEURODEGENERATIVE DISEASES (part 2)

items.title: Challenges in Developing Effective Prion Therapeutics
items.content: Current drugs that cross the blood-brain barrier can prolong life in mice with scrapie prions but not those replicating human CJD prions. Mutation rates for drug resistance in prions are likely near 100%, suggesting a need for multi-drug cocktails targeting various prion conformers.
keyPoints: Drugs that cross the blood-brain barrier can prolong life in mice with scrapie but not human CJD prions
Mutation rate for drug-resistant prions is likely ~100%
Effective therapeutics may require multi-drug cocktails targeting various prion conformers
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · PRIONS AND NEURODEGENERATIVE DISEASES (part 2)

items.title: Utility of iPSCs in Modeling Human Neurologic Diseases
items.content: Induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) enable the study of human neurologic diseases using patient-specific cells, overcoming limitations of animal models.
keyPoints: iPSC technology allows conversion of somatic cells into pluripotent stem cells using factors like Oct3/4, Klf-4, Sox2, and c-Myc.
Nonintegrating methods such as δendai virus, mRNA, or episomal vectors are used to avoid altering disease phenotypes.
iPSCs can be expanded indefinitely for deriving specific cell types relevant to neurologic diseases.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · NEURAL STEM CELL BIOLOGY

items.title: Advantages of iPSC Technology in Disease Study
items.content: iPSC technology facilitates research into monogenetic, sporadic, and complex polygenic disorders by creating genetically matched patient-specific cells.
keyPoints: Enables study of diseases with unidentified risk loci through patient-derived cell lines.
Allows exploration of disease phenotype variability based on genetic background.
Patient-specific iPSCs can model both heritable and sporadic forms of neurologic diseases.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · NEURAL STEM CELL BIOLOGY

items.title: Direct Reprogramming to Study Age-Related Neurologic Diseases
items.content: Direct reprogramming bypasses the epigenetic reset in iPSCs, preserving age-related signatures for studying diseases manifesting in older cells.
keyPoints: Uses specific transcription factors to convert somatic cells directly into neuron or glial cell types.
Preserves age-related epigenetic signatures, making it suitable for modeling late-onset neurologic disorders.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · NEURAL STEM CELL BIOLOGY

items.title: Variability in iPSC-derived Organoids for Nervous System Disease Modeling
items.content: In vitro models using patient-specific induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) face challenges due to variability in reprogramming and derivation protocols, affecting disease phenotype consistency. Validating phenotypes across multiple labs enhances confidence.
keyPoints: No standard reprogramming or derivation protocols for iPSCs
Variability in disease phenotypes reported by different laboratories
Validation of phenotypes across multiple labs increases specificity
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ORGANOIDS

items.title: Use of Isogenic Controls in Monogenic Disorders
items.content: For monogenic disorders, isogenic controls using gene editing (e.g., CRISPR-Cas9) can help create disease and control lines on an identical genetic background. However, variability in genetic backgrounds still necessitates comparing multiple patient lines to identify true disease phenotypes.
keyPoints: Isogenic controls possible only for monogenic disorders
Gene editing (e.g., CRISPR-Cas9) used to create identical genetic backgrounds
Comparison of multiple patient lines needed due to genetic background variability
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ORGANOIDS

items.title: Limitations and Advances in Brain Organoid Models
items.content: Brain organoids, derived from pluripotent stem cells, mimic early human brain development but remain at late fetal stages even after 6–9 months of culture. They lack key cell types like endothelial cells and microglia, though recent advances allow for the addition of these cells to create more complex models.
keyPoints: Organoids resemble early human brain development
Developmental stage comparable to late fetal stages after 6–9 months
Lack key cell types such as endothelial cells and microglia
Recent advances allow addition of vascular or immune cells
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ORGANOIDS

items.subtopic: BRAIN DEVELOPMENT AND DEVELOMENTAL DISORDERS: MICROCEPHALY AND LISSENCEPHALY
items.title: Utility of Cerebral Organoids in Modeling Microcephaly
items.content: Cerebral organoids derived from patient-derived iPSCs can model microcephaly by replicating disease phenotypes such as reduced progenitor cell proliferation and premature neural differentiation. This approach is particularly useful for studying disorders like MCPH, where mutations in genes like CDK5RAP2 affect brain development.
keyPoints: Cerebral organoids from iPSCs with CDK5RAP2 mutation show fewer proliferating progenitor cells and premature neural differentiation.
Introducing functional CDK5RAP2 can partially rescue the disease phenotype, indicating the importance of neural progenitor expansion in brain size.
Organoid size serves as a proxy for assessing microcephaly phenotypes.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · BRAIN DEVELOPMENT AND DEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: MICROCEPHALY AND LISSENCEPHALY

items.subtopic: BRAIN DEVELOPMENT AND DEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: MICROCEPHALY AND LISSENCEPHALY
items.title: Discovery of Novel Mechanisms in Brain Size Control Using Organoids
items.content: CRISPR-lineage tracing in organoids has identified novel mechanisms involved in brain size regulation, including pathways related to endoplasmic reticulum function and extracellular matrix production. This approach tested 173 candidate microcephaly genes.
keyPoints: A loss-of-function screen using CRISPR-lineage tracing was performed on 173 potential microcephaly genes.
Novel mechanisms for brain size control were discovered, including pathways involving endoplasmic reticulum function and extracellular matrix production.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · BRAIN DEVELOPMENT AND DEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: MICROCEPHALY AND LISSENCEPHALY

items.subtopic: BRAIN DEVELOPMENT AND DEVELOMENTAL DISORDERS: MICROCEPHALY AND LISSENCEPHALY
items.title: Modeling Lissencephaly with Cortical Organoids
items.content: Cortical organoids have been used to model lissencephaly, particularly Miller-Dieker syndrome (MDS), revealing unique neural progenitor cell phenotypes not observed in murine models. These include mitotic defects in outer radial glia cells and noncell autonomous Wnt signaling defects.
keyPoints: Cortical organoids model MDS with features like altered mitotic spindle orientation and neuronal migration defects.
Unique mitotic defect in outer radial glia cells (oRG) observed, which is specific to primates and not seen in mice.
Noncell autonomous Wnt signaling defects identified as an underlying mechanism in MDS.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · BRAIN DEVELOPMENT AND DEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: MICROCEPHALY AND LISSENCEPHALY

items.title: Zika Virus and Microcephaly: Role of Neural Progenitor Cells
items.content: Studies using human iPSC-derived neural progenitor cells demonstrated that Zika virus (ZIKV) has a specific tropism for these cells, leading to cell death and reduced organoid size. This finding supports the causal link between ZIKV infection and congenital microcephaly.
keyPoints: Zika virus infects human neural progenitor cells in vitro.
Infection leads to progenitor cell death and involution of organoid size.
Human iPSC-derived models were crucial in establishing the causal link between ZIKV and microcephaly.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ACQUIRED NEURODEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: ZIKA

items.title: Mechanisms of Zika Virus-Induced Microcephaly
items.content: Forebrain organoids have been used to identify the role of AXL in viral entry and TLR3 upregulation in response to ZIKV infection. These findings elucidate mechanisms contributing to microcephaly, including centrosomal abnormalities and altered mitotic cleavage planes leading to premature neural differentiation.
keyPoints: AXL is a flavivirus entry factor influencing ZIKV tropism.
Toll-like receptor 3 (TLR3) upregulation occurs in response to ZIKV infection.
Centrosomal abnormalities and altered mitotic cleavage planes are observed, leading to premature neural differentiation.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ACQUIRED NEURODEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: ZIKA

items.title: Fragile X Syndrome and iPSC Models
items.content: Fragile X syndrome is the most common heritable cause of intellectual disability, with a prevalence affecting 1 in 4000 males and 1 in 8000 females. The disorder arises from a CGG triplet repeat expansion in the FMR1 gene leading to epigenetic silencing and loss of fragile X mental retardation protein expression around the second trimester. iPSC-derived neurons model this condition, showing stunted neurites with decreased branching similar to findings in transgenic mouse models and patients.
keyPoints: Fragile X is a leading genetic cause of ASD.
Affects 1 in 4000 males and 1 in 8000 females.
Mutation: CGG triplet repeat expansion >200 in FMR1 gene.
Epigenetic silencing occurs around the beginning of the second trimester.
iPSC-derived neurons show stunted neurites with decreased branching.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · NEURODEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: AUTISM AND SCHIZOPHRENIA (part 1)

items.title: Rett Syndrome and Epigenetic Mechanisms
items.content: Rett syndrome is an X-linked neurodevelopmental disorder caused by mutations in the MECP2 gene, primarily affecting females due to X chromosome inactivation. Symptoms include microcephaly, developmental delay, autistic-like behaviors, seizures, and repetitive motor actions. iPSC models reveal that Rett neurons exhibit fewer dendritic spines, synapses, smaller cell bodies, reduced network activity, and altered inhibitory function due to low KCC2 expression.
keyPoints: Rett syndrome is caused by MECP2 gene mutations.
Primarily affects females; males with one copy often die in infancy.
Symptoms: microcephaly, developmental delay, autistic-like behaviors, seizures, repetitive motor actions.
iPSC models show fewer dendritic spines and synapses, smaller cell bodies, reduced network activity.
Altered inhibitory function due to low KCC2 expression.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · NEURODEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: AUTISM AND SCHIZOPHRENIA (part 1)

items.title: Utility of iPSC Models in Neurodevelopmental Disorders
items.content: iPSC-derived disease models are valuable for studying the pathophysiology and potential therapeutic interventions for neurodevelopmental disorders like Fragile X syndrome and Rett syndrome. These models replicate key features observed in patients, such as neurite stunting in Fragile X and synaptic deficits in Rett syndrome, offering a platform to screen for drugs or gene-editing strategies.
keyPoints: iPSC models replicate disease-specific neuronal features.
Used to study pathophysiology of disorders like Fragile X and Rett syndrome.
Potential for screening therapeutic agents or gene-editing strategies.
Replicate neurite stunting in Fragile X and synaptic deficits in Rett syndrome.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · NEURODEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: AUTISM AND SCHIZOPHRENIA (part 1)

items.title: Modeling Timothy Syndrome in iPSC-derived Organoids
items.content: Timothy syndrome, associated with ASD, is modeled using iPSC-derived organoids to study neuron defects. The disease results from a mutation in the CACNA1C gene affecting calcium channel activity. By fusing organoids representing different brain regions, researchers can observe interneuron migration and identify cell-autonomous migration defects.
keyPoints: Timothy syndrome is caused by a mutation in the CACNA1C gene coding for a voltage-gated calcium channel.
Neuron defects in Timothy syndrome organoids were rescued by selectively altering calcium channel activity.
Interneurons migrate from medial ganglionic eminence to neocortex, mimicking endogenous behavior.
A cell-autonomous migration defect was discovered in disease-carrying neurons.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · NEURODEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: AUTISM AND SCHIZOPHRENIA (part 2)

items.title: Challenges and Variability in iPSC Models of Neuropsychiatric Disorders
items.content: iPSC models are used to study ASD and schizophrenia, revealing diverse phenotypes. For ASD, variability includes accelerated cell cycles and excess inhibitory interneurons, contrasting with other models showing decreased spines and synapses. Schizophrenia studies show contradictory results, such as disruptions in adherens junctions or abnormal microRNA expression. The complexity of these disorders highlights the need for standardized protocols and rigorous identification methods.
keyPoints: iPSC models reveal diverse phenotypes in ASD, including accelerated cell cycles and excess inhibitory interneurons.
Contrasting findings: decreased spines and synapses in fragile X and Rett syndrome iPSC models versus exuberant neurite growth in large head size ASD cohort.
Schizophrenia studies show contradictory results like disruptions in adherens junctions or abnormal microRNA expression.
Standardized protocols and rigorous identification methods are needed to capture the true pathology of complex neurodevelopmental disorders.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · NEURODEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: AUTISM AND SCHIZOPHRENIA (part 2)

items.title: Understanding AD Pathogenesis and Modeling with Stem Cells
items.content: The amyloid hypothesis suggests that an imbalance in $\beta$-amyloid production and clearance leads to its accumulation, forming neuritic plaques and neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs) composed of hyperphosphorylated tau proteins. Familial AD is linked to mutations in genes like APP and presenilin 1/2. iPSC-derived neurons from patients with these mutations exhibit AD hallmarks such as intracellular $\beta$-amyloid and phosphorylated tau accumulation, which can be mitigated by secretase inhibitors or specific antibodies.
keyPoints: Amyloid hypothesis: imbalance in $\beta$-amyloid production/clearance
Neuritic plaques and NFTs formation from amyloid fibrils and hyperphosphorylated tau
Familial AD linked to mutations in APP, presenilin 1/2 genes
iPSC-derived neurons show AD hallmarks: intracellular $\beta$-amyloid, phosphorylated tau accumulation
Secretase inhibitors or antibodies can reduce disease features
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.title: Advancements in AD Modeling with Organoids and Early Detection
items.content: Three-dimensional organoid models better replicate AD pathologic features, including $\beta$-amyloid aggregation and NFTs formation, overcoming limitations of two-dimensional systems. These models may accelerate understanding of AD by enabling quasi in situ study of disease-carrying cells, potentially leading to novel therapeutic targets and biomarkers. Despite concerns about the age discrepancy between model development and typical AD onset, early signs like reduced hippocampal size and memory test scores are observed in genetically at-risk children as young as 3 years old.
keyPoints: Organoid models replicate AD features more accurately than two-dimensional systems
Potential for discovering new therapeutic targets and biomarkers
Concerns about age discrepancy between model development and typical AD onset
Early signs of AD observed in genetically at-risk children as young as 3 years old
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: CELL TYPE DISORDERS: ALS AND HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS
items.title: Mechanisms of Cell Vulnerability in ALS via iPSC Models
items.content: Induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC) models enable the study of specific neuron subtypes affected by diseases like ALS, providing insights into mechanisms of cell vulnerability and potential therapeutic strategies.
keyPoints: ALS affects spinal and cortical motor neurons specifically.
Stem cells allow unlimited production of diseased human motor neurons for research.
Toxic factors secreted by SOD1 astrocytes contribute to motor neuron death in ALS, indicating a non-cell-autonomous mechanism.
5–10% of ALS cases are familial with known mutations such as those in the SOD1 gene.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · CELL TYPE DISORDERS: ALS AND HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS

items.subtopic: CELL TYPE DISORDERS: ALS AND HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS
items.title: Potential of Stem Cell-Derived Models for ALS Drug Screening and Therapy Development
items.content: Stem cell-derived neurons, particularly motor neurons from iPSCs, serve as platforms to screen drugs and explore replacement therapies, aiming to address the specific vulnerabilities in ALS.
keyPoints: iPSC models can produce human midbrain dopaminergic neurons for PD, medium spiny striatal neurons for HD, and spinal/cortical motor neurons for ALS.
Stem cell-derived interneurons are spared in ALS models, highlighting the specific vulnerability of motor neurons.
Co-culture systems, organoids, and microfluidic models aim to replicate complex spinal cord environments for comprehensive ALS pathophysiology modeling.
Drug screening using human disease-expressing cells may identify therapeutic compounds.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · CELL TYPE DISORDERS: ALS AND HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE | AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS

items.title: Genetic Basis of Huntington's Disease Onset
items.content: The onset of Huntington's disease (HD) is influenced by the length of CAG triplet repeats in the huntingtin gene. Adult-onset HD typically occurs with 40–60 repeats, while juvenile-onset HD manifests with 60 or more repeats.
keyPoints: CAG repeat lengths determine disease onset: 40-60 for adult-onset, ≥60 for juvenile-onset
HD is dominantly inherited and leads to cognitive decline and motor dysfunction starting in the third to fifth decade of life
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE

items.title: Cellular Mechanisms and Vulnerability in HD
items.content: Mutant huntingtin protein accumulates and misfolds, forming aggregates that lead to neuronal death. Medium spiny neurons of the striatum are particularly vulnerable, though widespread neuron loss occurs.
keyPoints: Excess huntingtin protein forms aggregates causing metabolic toxicity and cell death
Medium spiny neurons in the striatum are most affected, but cortical involvement complicates treatment strategies
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · HUNTINGTON'S DISEASE

items.title: Challenges in Modeling Neurodegenerative Diseases with Human iPSCs
items.content: Human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) face challenges in modeling neurodegenerative diseases due to the slow progression of these conditions and the time required for neuronal differentiation. Accelerating degenerative phenotypes may be possible through gene editing or aging-associated stresses.
keyPoints: Neuro-degenerative disease changes progress slowly, often starting later in life.
Differentiation and maturation of human neurons from stem cells take months.
Gene editing or application of DNA-damaging agents/proteasome inhibitors may accelerate degenerative phenotypes.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · FUTURE PERSPECTIVES

items.title: Advancements in Brain Organoid Models for Neurological Research
items.content: Three-dimensional brain organoids offer a more complex environment to study disease processes like extracellular protein aggregation. Innovations such as regionalized organoids and bioengineered scaffolds are enhancing their fidelity, though challenges remain in replicating mature brain architecture.
keyPoints: Two-dimensional cultures lack the complexity needed for certain disease models.
Three-dimensional organoids better model complex environments.
Regionalized organoids created using microfluidic chambers and morphogen gradients like BMPs and WNTs.
Bioengineered scaffolds may allow growth of larger structures, addressing necrotic core issues.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · FUTURE PERSPECTIVES

items.subtopic: A CURRENT PERSPECTIVE ON NEURAL STEM CELLS IN THE CLINIC | PARKINSON'S DISEASE
items.title: Stem Cell Therapy for Parkinson's Disease: Progress and Challenges
items.content: The pursuit of stem cell therapies for Parkinson's Disease (PD) has seen both progress and setbacks. While initial trials using fetal-derived midbrain cells showed limited success, recent efforts focus on induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and embryonic stem cells to derive dopamine neurons. These newer approaches aim to overcome previous limitations such as cell purity and quantity.
keyPoints: Initial trials with fetal-derived midbrain cells in the 1990s showed limited success, with only a small number of younger patients benefiting.
Dyskinesias were linked to serotonergic neurons inadvertently included in grafts; stem cell protocols aim to avoid this by providing purer dopamine neuron populations.
A phase 1/2 clinical trial using iPSC-derived dopamine neurons began in Japan in 2018, with ~5 million cells transplanted bilaterally into the putamen of seven patients.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · A CURRENT PERSPECTIVE ON NEURAL STEM CELLS IN THE CLINIC | PARKINSON'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: A CURRENT PERSPECTIVE ON NEURAL STEM CELLS IN THE CLINIC | PARKINSON'S DISEASE
items.title: Regulatory and Safety Considerations in Stem Cell Trials for PD
items.content: Stem cell trials for Parkinson's Disease are subject to rigorous regulatory scrutiny to ensure safety. The FDA has approved the first U.S. trial using stem cell-derived dopamine neurons, while European trials also assess safety and tolerability over extended periods.
keyPoints: The FDA approved a phase 1 clinical trial in the U.S. for stem cell-derived dopamine neurons derived from an embryonic stem cell line.
A European trial began in 2023 with four patients receiving low doses of embryonic stem cell-derived dopaminergic neurons, followed for 36 months to evaluate safety and tolerability.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · A CURRENT PERSPECTIVE ON NEURAL STEM CELLS IN THE CLINIC | PARKINSON'S DISEASE

items.subtopic: A CURRENT PERSPECTIVE ON NEURAL STEM CELLS IN THE CLINIC | PARKINSON'S DISEASE
items.title: Challenges in Stem Cell Therapy Enrollment and Efficacy for PD
items.content: Despite advancements, stem cell therapy trials for Parkinson's Disease face challenges such as slow enrollment and unmet efficacy expectations. These issues highlight the need for improved techniques and patient recruitment strategies.
keyPoints: A European trial of fetal cell transplantation for PD was terminated due to slow enrollment and lack of clear clinical improvement.
The Japanese iPSC trial began in 2018 with a focus on safety and possible efficacy, transplanting ~5 million cells into seven patients.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · A CURRENT PERSPECTIVE ON NEURAL STEM CELLS IN THE CLINIC | PARKINSON'S DISEASE

items.title: Understanding Batten Disease: A Phase 1 Trial Approach with Neural Stem Cells
items.content: Batten disease is an autosomal recessive metabolic disorder characterized by the inability to synthesize a critical lysosomal enzyme for brain function. The phase 1 trial involved six patients receiving neural stem cells derived from fetal tissue, aiming to deliver the missing enzyme. While no adverse events were reported and cell safety was confirmed, there was no clinical improvement or evidence of cell survival.
keyPoints: Batten disease is an autosomal recessive metabolic disorder.
The phase 1 trial involved six patients with infantile and late-infantile forms.
Neural stem cells were used as a delivery device for the missing lysosomal enzyme.
No adverse events reported, but no clinical improvement or evidence of cell survival.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · BATTEN'S DISEASE AND PELIZAEUS-MERZBACHER DISEASE

items.title: Pelizaeus-Merzbacher Disease: Neural Stem Cell Therapy Potential
items.content: Pelizaeus-Merzbacher disease (PMD) is an inherited fatal dysmyelination syndrome. The company pursued neural stem cell therapy for PMD after a phase 1 trial in Batten disease, leveraging the cells' neurogenic and gliogenic potential to promote myelin formation. Experimental animal studies showed that most persisting cells became oligodendrocytes when delivered to white matter regions.
keyPoints: Pelizaeus-Merzbacher disease is an inherited fatal dysmyelination syndrome.
Neural stem cells have neurogenic and gliogenic potential.
Experimental studies showed cell transformation into oligodendrocytes in white matter.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · BATTEN'S DISEASE AND PELIZAEUS-MERZBACHER DISEASE

items.title: Stem Cell Trials for Spinal Cord Injury: Current Status and Challenges
items.content: Numerous early-stage clinical trials are exploring stem cell treatments, particularly mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) and hematopoietic stem cells, for spinal cord injuries. Despite anecdotal successes, most studies lack robust blinding and control mechanisms. The rationale behind using MSCs is not well understood, with proposed benefits including trophic support and immune modulation.
keyPoints: Over 1 million patients worldwide suffer from spinal cord injuries.
Most trials are unblinded and involve small patient numbers.
Proposed mechanisms for MSC use include vague concepts like trophic support and immune modulation.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · SPINAL CORD INJURY (part 1)

items.title: Regulatory Challenges in Stem Cell Therapy Trials
items.content: The development of stem cell therapies faces significant regulatory hurdles. For instance, a trial using pluripotent embryonic stem cells to generate oligodendrocytes was initially stalled due to concerns over abnormal growths but later resumed under new leadership. Additionally, Japan's CTL approval process aims to expedite the availability of regenerative medical products by allowing conditional marketing based on safety data.
keyPoints: Concerns over abnormal growths can stall stem cell trials.
Japan's 2014 CTL approval allows for conditional marketing when efficacy is not fully established.
CTL approval requires postmarketing data to demonstrate clinical benefit within 7 years.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · SPINAL CORD INJURY (part 1)

items.title: Current Clinical Trials and Future Directions in Stem Cell Therapy
items.content: A phase 1/2a multicenter trial is currently testing escalating doses of stem cells for subacute severe cervical spinal cord injuries, with no adverse events reported over two years. A later-stage comparative clinical trial is planned to assess efficacy further. In Japan, a separate trial using iPSC-derived neural stem and progenitor cells has begun but results are pending.
keyPoints: Phase 1/2a trial involves 25 patients with subacute severe cervical spinal cord injuries.
No adverse events reported for 21 patients at 2 years posttreatment.
Japan's trial using iPSC-derived neural stem cells is ongoing, but results are not yet available.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · SPINAL CORD INJURY (part 1)

items.title: Efficacy and Approval of Stemirac
items.content: Stemirac, a treatment using autologous bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stem cells, was conditionally approved in Japan for spinal cord injury. In an open-label trial with 13 patients, 12 showed improvement by at least one level on the American Spinal Injury Association impairment scale six months post-treatment.
keyPoints: Stemirac received conditional approval from Japan's health ministry in 2018.
Trial involved 13 patients with severe spinal cord injury treated one month after injury.
12 out of 13 patients improved by at least one level on the American Spinal Injury Association impairment scale six months post-treatment.
Concerns exist due to lack of blinding, control group, and definitive proof of efficacy in a small trial.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · SPINAL CORD INJURY (part 2)

items.title: Ethical Concerns with Stemirac
items.content: The approval of Stemirac raised ethical concerns due to its conditional status based on an unblinded, uncontrolled study without proven efficacy. Patients in Japan are being charged for this treatment while further trials to test efficacy continue.
keyPoints: Stemirac's approval was based on a small, unblinded, and uncontrolled trial.
Concerns about charging patients for an unproven therapy while efficacy trials are ongoing.
Ethical issues highlighted by the stem cell community regarding risk without proven benefit.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · SPINAL CORD INJURY (part 2)

items.title: Challenges in Stem Cell Therapy for ALS
items.content: Stem cell therapy for ALS faces significant challenges due to the complexity of integrating new neurons into existing spinal cord circuits and addressing both upper and lower motor neuron deficits.
keyPoints: Integration of new neurons requires long axons that take months to years to project to targets.
Unique segmental identity of spinal motor neurons necessitates a range of molecular identities for replacement cells.
Diseased astrocytes and microglia may produce toxic effects on replacement cells.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS

items.title: GDNF-Producing Progenitor Cells in ALS Treatment
items.content: A clinical trial involving GDNF-producing progenitor cells showed no statistically significant slowing of disease progression, with some patients developing schwannomas.
keyPoints: Phase 1/2a trial completed in 2019; FDA approved in 2016.
Grafting into lumbar spinal cord did not show statistically significant results.
Schwannomas developed due to off-target GDNF secretion.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS

items.title: Importance of Phase 3 Trials in ALS Stem Cell Therapies
items.content: Phase 3 trials are crucial for determining the efficacy of stem cell therapies, as evidenced by BrainStorm's experience with MSC therapy.
keyPoints: BrainStorm's phase 3 trial showed no significant clinical improvement.
FDA advisory committee voted against approval in 2023.
Emphasizes need for rigorous trials to validate therapeutic claims.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · AMYOTROPHEC LATERAL SCLEROSIS

items.title: Stem Cell-Derived Inhibitory Interneurons for Temporal Lobe Epilepsy
items.content: A clinical trial initiated in 2022 is exploring the use of stem cell-derived inhibitory interneurons to treat medically intractable temporal lobe epilepsy. These interneurons, expressing somatostatin (SST), are derived from an embryonic stem cell line and have shown efficacy in a mouse model. The trial involves delivering these cells surgically to the epileptic hippocampus of 10 patients with confirmed seizure focus there. Patients receive anti-rejection therapy for 12 months post-treatment, and outcomes will be monitored over two years.
keyPoints: Phase 1/2 clinical trial launched in 2022
Stem cell-derived SST-expressing inhibitory interneurons used
10 patients with hippocampal seizure focus treated
Patients followed for 2 years post-treatment
Anti-rejection therapy administered for the first 12 months
Potential to reduce seizures and preserve/improve cognitive function
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · EPILEPSY | MACULAR DEGENERATION

items.title: Challenges in iPSC-Derived Cell Therapy for Age-Related Macular Degeneration
items.content: The use of induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) for generating patient-specific autologous retinal pigment epithelial (RPE) cells aims to treat age-related macular degeneration. The Japanese government has supported the development of iPSC banks to match therapies with individual HLA haplotypes, minimizing immune rejection. However, a trial was halted after discovering an oncogenic mutation in a patient-derived RPE cell line, leading to a shift towards using banked iPSC lines for therapy.
keyPoints: iPSC technology enables patient-specific autologous cell generation
First Japanese study approved in 2013 targeting macular degeneration
Challenge: Coaxing replacement cells to form epithelium in subretinal space
Trial halted due to oncogenic mutation discovery in patient-derived RPE cells
Shift to using banked iPSC lines with extensive gene sequencing and quality controls
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · EPILEPSY | MACULAR DEGENERATION

items.title: Understanding MSCs' Role in Regenerative Therapy
items.content: Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) have been explored for their potential in regenerative therapy. Initially, they were thought to be pluripotent and capable of differentiating into various cell types including nerve and heart muscle. However, it was later discovered that MSCs are not pluripotent. Instead, their therapeutic effects are attributed to the release of trophic factors, cytokines, or inflammatory modulators.
keyPoints: MSCs sourced from bone marrow, peripheral blood, adipose tissue, umbilical cord, and other sites.
Initial reports claimed MSCs were pluripotent; later found not to be.
Therapeutic effects attributed to trophic factors, cytokines, or inflammatory modulators.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MESENCHYMAL STEM CELLS

items.title: Risks and Regulatory Challenges in MSC Therapy
items.content: The ease of harvesting MSCs from blood or adipose tissue has led to numerous clinical trials for various conditions. However, lax regulation, especially for autologous cell therapies, has resulted in unregulated clinics making unsubstantiated claims. This has led to patient harm and fatalities, highlighting the need for cautious evaluation and regulation.
keyPoints: Rapid expansion of clinical trials due to ease of MSC extraction from blood or adipose tissue.
Autologous cell therapy often escapes FDA regulation if cells are not significantly processed.
Unregulated clinics have caused patient harm; three patients became blind following treatment in a Florida clinic.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MESENCHYMAL STEM CELLS

items.subtopic: MSCS FOR TRAUMATIC BRAIN INJURY | PERSPECTIVE
items.title: Use of MSCs in Traumatic Brain Injury: Mechanism and Efficacy
items.content: An allogeneic bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) line was approved in Japan for improving chronic motor paralysis from traumatic brain injury. The MSCs were engineered to express the human Notch-1 intracellular domain, enhancing their nerve regeneration capabilities by promoting FDF-2 secretion.
keyPoints: Conditional marketing approval received in Japan in 2024.
Engineered MSCs for enhanced nerve cell regeneration via Notch-1 and FDF-2.
Phase 2 clinical trial involved 46 patients with moderate to severe TBI.
Significant improvement noted on the FMMS at 24 weeks (p = .04).
Sham group of 15 showed small improvements, indicating placebo effect.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MSCS FOR TRAUMATIC BRAIN INJURY | PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: MSCS FOR TRAUMATIC BRAIN INJURY | PERSPECTIVE
items.title: Future Directions in Stem Cell Therapy for Neurologic Disorders
items.content: The potential of stem cell-based therapies for neurologic diseases is promising, with ongoing pivotal double-blind controlled trials. The success of these therapies will depend on the outcomes of these studies.
keyPoints: Stem cell biology may revolutionize regenerative medicine for neurological disorders.
Current focus includes PD, spinal cord injury, ALS, and epilepsy.
Outcomes of first wave of pivotal double-blind controlled trials are crucial.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MSCS FOR TRAUMATIC BRAIN INJURY | PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases | NEUROIMMUNOLOGY AND NEUROINFLAMMATION
items.question: Which cell types are recognized as central players in a wide range of brain disorders due to their role in mediating tissue damage?
items.explanation: Microglia/macrophages, astrocytes, and oligodendrocytes are central to mediating tissue damage in brain disorders.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases | NEUROIMMUNOLOGY AND NEUROINFLAMMATION

items.subtopic: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases | NEUROIMMUNOLOGY AND NEUROINFLAMMATION
items.question: What is the role of neuroinflammation and the innate immune system in neurological disorders?
options: They are only involved in autoimmune diseases.
They play key roles in an expanded category of neurologic disorders, including neurodegenerative disorders.
They have no significant impact on neurologic disorders.
They primarily affect peripheral nervous system disorders.
items.explanation: Neuroinflammation and the innate immune system are key in a broad category of neurological disorders, particularly neurodegenerative ones.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases | NEUROIMMUNOLOGY AND NEUROINFLAMMATION

items.subtopic: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases | NEUROIMMUNOLOGY AND NEUROINFLAMMATION
items.question: What is the significance of communication networks between cell types in the CNS?
options: They are irrelevant to human CNS disorders.
They contribute to non-cell-autonomous pathologies underlying many human CNS disorders.
They only affect neuroplasticity and rehabilitation.
items.explanation: Communication networks between cell types in the CNS are likely to underlie many human CNS disorders through non-cell-autonomous pathologies.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases | NEUROIMMUNOLOGY AND NEUROINFLAMMATION

items.question: Which receptor do most microglia rely on for survival signals?
options: Triggering receptor expressed on myeloid cells 2 (TREM2)
items.explanation: Most microglia receive survival signals through the colony-stimulating factor 1 receptor (Csf1r).

items.question: What is a primary role of microglia in neurodegenerative conditions according to genetic evidence?
options: Inducing proliferation and migration of neurons
items.explanation: Genetic evidence supports a primary role for microglia in regulating innate immune processes.

items.question: What is the consequence of knocking out C1q or C3 in neurodegeneration?
options: Rescue of clinical and pathologic abnormalities
items.explanation: Knockout of C1q or C3 can rescue the clinical and pathologic abnormalities associated with neurodegeneration.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROGLIA AND MACROPHAGES (part 1)

items.question: Which molecule is involved in tagging synapses for elimination by microglia?
items.explanation: Synapses targeted for elimination are tagged by the complement proteins, including C3.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROGLIA AND MACROPHAGES (part 1)

items.question: Which gene mutation is associated with frontotemporal degeneration due to haploinsufficiency of progranulin?
items.explanation: Mutations in the GRN gene lead to reduced levels of progranulin, causing frontotemporal degeneration.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROGLIA AND MACROPHAGES (part 2)

items.question: What is a potential therapeutic target for neurodegeneration due to progranulin deficiency?
items.explanation: Clinical trials are exploring a monoclonal antibody to block the lysosomal degradation of progranulin.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROGLIA AND MACROPHAGES (part 2)

items.question: What role do microglia play in antigen presentation within the brain?
options: They express class II MHC molecules to present antigens to CD4 T cells
They primarily recruit memory CD8 T cells via CCL3 secretion
They are derived from hematogenous monocytes under inflammatory conditions
They initiate inflammatory demyelination in EAE
items.explanation: Microglia express class II MHC molecules, enabling them to present antigens to CD4 T cells.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROGLIA AND MACROPHAGES (part 2)

items.question: Which factor secreted by astrocytes is involved in regulating synapse development, maintenance, and pruning?
items.explanation: Astrocytes secrete factors such as apolipoprotein E, thrombospondins, and glypicans that regulate synapse development, maintenance, and pruning.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ASTROCYTES

items.question: What role do astrocytes play in the regulation of vascular tone within the CNS?
items.explanation: Astrocytes regulate vascular tone partly through astrocyte-astrocyte communication mediated by gap junctions and calcium waves.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ASTROCYTES

items.question: In the context of neuroimmune disorders such as MS, what role do astrocytes play?
options: Promote survival and activation of pathogenic autoreactive B cells
Limit inflammation through secretion of IL-27
items.explanation: In MS, astrocytes produce BAFF within lesions that promote the survival and activation of pathogenic autoreactive B cells.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · ASTROCYTES

items.question: What is the primary function of myelin in nerve conduction?
options: Myelin decreases impulse speed by insulating axons.
Myelin speeds up impulse conduction by allowing action potentials to jump between nodes of Ranvier.
Myelin prevents any form of electrical signal from passing through the axon.
Myelin increases the diameter of axons without affecting impulse conduction.
items.explanation: Myelin speeds up nerve impulse conduction by allowing action potentials to jump between nodes of Ranvier, a process known as saltatory conduction.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · OLIGODENDROCYTES AND MYELIN

items.question: Which cell type typically myelinates multiple axons in the CNS?
items.explanation: In the CNS, a single oligodendrocyte usually ensheaths multiple axons.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · OLIGODENDROCYTES AND MYELIN

items.question: What is the role of Wnt pathway signaling in OPCs?
options: It regulates OPC attachment and retention to the vasculature.
It has no effect on OPC behavior.
items.explanation: Wnt pathway signaling regulates OPC migration along endothelial cells, aiding in their attachment and retention to the vasculature.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · OLIGODENDROCYTES AND MYELIN

items.question: Which molecule is inhibitory to oligodendrocyte differentiation?
items.explanation: LINGO-1 is one of the molecules that inhibit oligodendrocyte differentiation and myelination.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · OLIGODENDROCYTES AND MYELIN

items.question: Which system is primarily responsible for the clearance of debris from the brain interstitium?
items.explanation: The glymphatic system is responsible for removing waste products from the brain interstitium.

items.question: What role do aquaporin-4 water channels play in the glymphatic system?
options: They are involved in arterial propulsion of CSF.
They aid in astrocyte uptake of CSF.
items.explanation: Aquaporin-4 water channels aid in the movement of CSF into astrocytes, facilitating interstitial fluid flow.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · LYMPHATICS OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.subtopic: LYMPHATICS OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM
items.question: What is a consequence of knocking out aquaporin-4 in mice?
options: Reduced interstitial fluid flow in the brain.
Enhanced immune response in the CNS.
items.explanation: Knocking out aquaporin-4 markedly reduces interstitial fluid flow in the brain, highlighting its critical role.

items.question: Which factor accelerates glymphatic transport of fluids and solutes?
items.explanation: The transport of fluids and solutes in the glymphatic system accelerates with sleep.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · LYMPHATICS OF THE CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM

items.question: Which cytokines are increased in mice raised in a germ-free environment, contributing to resistance against EAE?
items.explanation: Mice raised in germ-free environments show increased production of the immunosuppressive cytokines IL-10 and IL-13.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROBIOTA AND NEUROLOGIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What is a proposed mechanism by which microbial proteins can trigger immune responses against nervous system components?
items.explanation: Molecular mimicry is a mechanism where microbial proteins trigger immune responses against similar nervous system components.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · MICROBIOTA AND NEUROLOGIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.reason: This method involves using small-molecule inhibitors to block endoderm and mesodermal cell fates, creating neural cells by default.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the process of dual-SMAD inhibition.

items.assertion: Directed protocols can be used to reduce variability in brain organoids.
items.reason: These protocols restrict outcomes to more defined brain regions like the forebrain or cortex.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how directed protocols help reduce variability.

items.assertion: Organoids can fully replicate all aspects of human brain development.
items.reason: They contain a diversity of progenitor and neuronal cell types topographically distributed within ventricular regions.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because organoids only partially reflect organizational features, while the reason is true regarding their cellular composition.

items.subtopic: BRAIN DEVELOPMENT AND DEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: MICROCEPHALY AND LISSENCEPHALY
items.assertion: Cortical organoids are useful in modeling human brain development disorders.
items.reason: They can replicate early prenatal developmental events such as neural progenitor cell proliferation and neuronal migration.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why cortical organoids are useful.

items.subtopic: BRAIN DEVELOPMENT AND DEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: MICROCEPHALY AND LISSENCEPHALY
items.assertion: The study of microcephaly using cerebral organoids has not provided any new information.
items.reason: Cerebral organoids derived from patients with microcephaly did not reveal new insights or disease features of CDK5RAP2 microcephaly that had not already been described in mouse models.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why no new information was provided.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · BRAIN DEVELOPMENT AND DEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: MICROCEPHALY AND LISSENCEPHALY

items.assertion: The causal link between Zika virus and congenital microcephaly was established by epidemiological studies alone.
items.reason: Human iPSC-derived neural progenitor cells and organoids demonstrated ZIKV tropism for human neural progenitor cells.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the causal link was supported by studies using human iPSC-derived models, not epidemiological studies alone.

items.assertion: Fragile X iPSC-derived neurons can be used to study disease pathogenesis.
items.reason: These neurons reproduce features observed in transgenic mouse models and patients, increasing confidence in the model.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why iPSC-derived neurons are useful for studying disease pathogenesis.

items.assertion: Rett syndrome is caused by overexpression of the MECP2 gene.
items.reason: The pathophysiology involves abnormal epigenetic regulation leading to decreased transcriptional repression of genes.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; Rett syndrome is caused by a mutation in the MECP2 gene, not overexpression. The reason correctly describes the involvement of epigenetic regulation.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · NEURODEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: AUTISM AND SCHIZOPHRENIA (part 1)

items.assertion: The clinical features of most neuropsychiatric diseases reflect disorders in processes such as circuit formation and refinement that occur after birth.
items.reason: These processes may be difficult to capture at the fetal stage of development reflected in stem cell models.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these features might not be captured in fetal-stage stem cell models.
items.reference: Pathobiology of Neurologic Diseases · NEURODEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS: AUTISM AND SCHIZOPHRENIA (part 2)

items.assertion: Juvenile-onset Huntington's disease iPSCs express robust disease phenotypes early.
items.reason: These iPSCs have the longest CAG repeat lengths, which are associated with juvenile onset.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why juvenile-onset disease iPSCs express robust phenotypes.

items.assertion: The dyskinesias in the NIH trials were due to an abundance of serotonergic neurons.
items.reason: Protocols for deriving dopaminergic neurons from stem cells would avoid this complication by providing a more purified cell population.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains why using stem cells might prevent dyskinesias.

items.reason: Four patients treated with a low dose experienced no acute adverse effects.
